snips.name: chassis-port-config
snips.topic: Chassis port-speed configuration for QFX5220/5130/5700 spine and border leaf
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — port speed assignments follow Apstra intent model.
All ports explicitly set to $PORT_SPEED; Apstra requires explicit speed even when it matches the hardware default.
QFX5220 spine uses 16 ports; QFX5130 border leaf uses 32+2 ports with breakout capability on ports 8–19.
Platform mapping: QFX5220-32CD and QFX5130-32CD use et-0/0/N; QFX5700 uses et-0/0/N with dual-RE chassis redundancy block; ACX7100-48L uses et-0/0/N; PTX10001-36MR uses et-0/0/N.

pairWith.raw: evo/interfaces/fabric-uplink.conf  (ports configured here carry the fabric links)
pairWith.note: ports configured here carry the fabric links

snips.bodyHtml:
chassis {
    fpc $FPC_SLOT {
        pic $PIC_SLOT {
            port $PORT_NUM {
                speed $PORT_SPEED;
            }
            /* repeat for each port in range */
        }
    }
}

snips.name: grpc-certificate
snips.topic: gRPC TLS certificate for Juniper Apstra controller authentication
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — the certificate name "aos_grpc" is the Apstra convention (AOS = Apstra Operating System, the former product name).
SECRET-DATA placeholder replaces the actual PEM-encoded cert+key at commit time.
The certificate is referenced by the gRPC extension-service under system services.
snips.bodyHtml:
security {
    certificates {
        local {
            $CERT_NAME {
                "$SECRET_DATA"; ## SECRET-DATA
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: external-vlan-tagged
snips.topic: Flexible-VLAN-tagged external breakout interface for inter-VRF/WAN exit
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — border leaf external connectivity uses a 400G port broken into 4×10G sub-ports; only sub-port :0 is wired to the external router.
flexible-vlan-tagging enables multiple VLAN units on a single physical sub-port, one unit per VRF (blue=399, red=299 in this JVD).
native-vlan-id 1 ensures untagged frames are handled correctly.

Platform interface mapping: QFX5130-32CD: et-0/0/8:0 (breakout) QFX5700: et-0/0/8:0 (breakout) ACX7100-48L: et-0/0/8:0 (breakout) PTX10001-36MR: et-0/0/8:0 (breakout) QFX5120-32C (Junos): xe-0/0/0 (native flexible-vlan, no breakout)

pairWith.raw: evo/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf         (L3 units defined on this interface)
pairWith.note: L3 units defined on this interface

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $PARENT_PORT {
        description "Breakout $PARENT_PORT";
        number-of-sub-ports $NUM_SUB_PORTS;
        speed $BREAKOUT_SPEED;
    }
    $EXT_INTF {
        description to.mx-external;
        flexible-vlan-tagging;
        native-vlan-id $NATIVE_VLAN;
    }
}

snips.name: fabric-uplink
snips.topic: Point-to-point fabric uplink to spine with /31 IPv4 addressing
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — description follows Apstra "facing_<peer>:<port>" convention.
MTU 9216 at physical layer, 9170 at IP layer (46 bytes reserved for L2 overhead).
IPv4 /31 point-to-point addressing (RFC 3021) — no broadcast waste.

Platform interface mapping: QFX5220-32CD spine: et-0/0/0 through et-0/0/15 QFX5130-32CD border leaf: et-0/0/2, et-0/0/3 (spine-facing) ACX7100-48L: et-0/0/2, et-0/0/3 PTX10001-36MR: et-0/0/2, et-0/0/3 QFX5700: et-0/0/2, et-0/0/3

pairWith.raw: evo/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf  (BGP sessions run over these links)
pairWith.note: BGP sessions run over these links

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $FABRIC_INTF {
        description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
        speed 100g;
        mtu $LINK_MTU;
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                mtu $IP_MTU;
                address $LOCAL_IP_SLASH31;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: irb-gateway
snips.topic: IRB gateway interface for EVPN/VXLAN symmetric routing (ERB model)
highlights: Border leaf IRB pattern — in the 3-stage ERB design, EVO border leafs use IRB units on the external VRF breakout interfaces rather than on per-VLAN fabric segments (those live on the Junos leaf tier).
The border leaf variant uses the external-vlan-tagged interface as the L3 gateway rather than a vlans/vxlan-mapped IRB; see external-vlan-tagged.conf.
Dual-stack addressing with per-VRF unit assignment.
snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $EXT_INTF {
        unit $VLAN_UNIT {
            description "$UNIT_DESC";
            vlan-id $VLAN_ID;
            family inet {
                address $UNIT_IPV4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $UNIT_IPV6;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: loopback-multi-unit
snips.topic: Multi-unit loopback for router-id, EVPN overlay, and per-VRF identities
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — unit allocation follows Apstra ID assignment.
Unit 0: router-id + underlay reachability (used as BGP overlay local-address).
Units 2+: per-VRF loopback for route-distinguisher source (one unit per tenant VRF).
Spine has only unit 0 (no VRFs); border leaf adds units for each VRF (blue, red).
Both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses assigned for dual-stack EVPN signaling.

pairWith.raw: evo/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf (VRF references lo0 unit for RD)
pairWith.note: VRF references lo0 unit for RD

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    lo0 {
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                address $ROUTER_ID_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $ROUTER_ID_V6;
            }
        }
        unit $VRF_UNIT {
            family inet {
                address $VRF_LO_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $VRF_LO_V6;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: ipv6-router-advertisement
snips.topic: IPv6 Router Advertisement on IRB/WAN interfaces
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — RA for external IPv6 interfaces.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    router-advertisement {
        interface $INTERFACE {
            max-advertisement-interval 30;
            min-advertisement-interval 10;
        }
        /* ... repeat per IPv6-enabled external interface ... */
    }
}

snips.name: l2-learning-telemetry
snips.topic: L2 learning telemetry for remote MAC entry visibility
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — remote MAC telemetry.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    l2-learning {
        telemetry {
            enable-remote-entries;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: lldp-discovery
snips.topic: LLDP neighbor discovery configuration
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — LLDP for topology discovery.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    lldp {
        port-id-subtype interface-name;
        port-description-type interface-description;
        neighbour-port-info-display port-id;
        interface all;
    }
}

snips.name: rstp-bridge
snips.topic: RSTP bridge configuration with edge-port BPDU blocking
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Spine has `protocols rstp { disable; }` (no L2 on spine).
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    rstp {
        bridge-priority 0;
        bpdu-block-on-edge;
    }
}

snips.name: sflow-telemetry
snips.topic: sFlow telemetry sampling and collector configuration
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — sFlow for traffic telemetry.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    sflow {
        polling-interval 20;
        sample-rate {
            ingress 128;
            egress 128;
        }
        source-ip $SOURCE_IP;
        collector $COLLECTOR_IP {
            udp-port $COLLECTOR_PORT;
        }
        interfaces $INTERFACE;
        /* ... repeat per monitored interface ... */
    }
}

snips.name: bgp-aos-export-policy
snips.topic: BGP-AOS-Policy master export filter chaining AllPodNetworks and BGP redistribution
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — master export policy for all fabric BGP sessions.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-20 {
            from protocol bgp;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-20 {
            from protocol bgp;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: community-definitions
snips.topic: BGP community definitions for fabric signaling and route tagging
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — community values are OS-independent.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    /* Base communities — present on all devices */
    community DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4 members [ 3:20007 21001:26000 ];
    community DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6 members [ 3:20008 21001:26000 ];
    community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER members 0:14;
    community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER members 0:15;
    community FABRIC_COMMUNITIES members [ 0:12 0:13 0:14 0:15 .+:200.. 2....:260.. ];

    /* Per-VRF communities — one pair (v4 + v6) per VRF on leaf/border-leaf */
    community $VRF_COMM_V4 members $VRF_MEMBERS_V4;
    community $VRF_COMM_V6 members $VRF_MEMBERS_V6;

    /* External-peer import communities — border-leaf only */
    community RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME members [ 3:20009 $VRF_ID:26000 ];
    community RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME members [ 3:20010 $VRF_ID:26000 ];
}

snips.name: external-route-export
snips.topic: External route export policy with fabric community stripping
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — strips fabric communities before external export.

pairWith.raw: evo/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf     (VRF BGP applies this as export)
pairWith.note: VRF BGP applies this as export

snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                route-filter-list RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community delete FABRIC_COMMUNITIES;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                route-filter-list RoutesToExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community delete FABRIC_COMMUNITIES;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-30 {
            from family inet;
            then reject;
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-40 {
            from family inet6;
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: external-route-import
snips.topic: External route import policy with route-filter-list and community tagging
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — import policy for external VRF BGP peers.

pairWith.raw: evo/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf     (VRF BGP applies this as import)
pairWith.note: VRF BGP applies this as import

snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                route-filter-list RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community add $IMPORT_COMMUNITY;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                route-filter-list RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community add $IMPORT_COMM_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-30 {
            from family inet;
            then reject;
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-40 {
            from family inet6;
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: leaf-to-spine-evpn-filter
snips.topic: LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT loop-prevention filter rejecting spine-originated EVPN routes
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — loop prevention for eBGP CLOS EVPN overlay.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT {
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT-10 {
            from {
                protocol bgp;
                community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER;
            }
            then reject;
        }
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT-20 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: leaf-to-spine-fabric-filter
snips.topic: LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT loop-prevention filter rejecting spine-originated fabric routes
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — underlay loop prevention for eBGP CLOS.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT {
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT-10 {
            from {
                protocol bgp;
                community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER;
            }
            then reject;
        }
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT-20 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: per-packet-load-balance
snips.topic: Per-packet load-balance policy for PFE forwarding-table export
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — identical on all devices regardless of role or OS.
"per-packet" means per-flow hash-based ECMP (not true per-packet round-robin).
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement PFE-LB {
        then {
            load-balance per-packet;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: pod-network-export
snips.topic: AllPodNetworks direct-route export policy with per-VRF community tagging
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — per-VRF variants tag direct routes for EVPN ip-prefix-routes export filtering.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks {
        term AllPodNetworks-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME {
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $VRF_COMMUNITY_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $VRF_COMMUNITY_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: route-filter-lists
snips.topic: Route-filter-list definitions for external peer import/export control
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — route-filter-list syntax is OS-independent.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    /* Import filter — accept only default route from external peer */
    route-filter-list RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0.0.0.0/0 exact;
    }
    route-filter-list RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0::0/0 exact;
    }

    /* Export filter — advertise specific internal prefixes to external peer */
    route-filter-list RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0.0.0.0/0 prefix-length-range /1-/32;
        $PREFIX/$LEN upto /32;
        /* ... additional site-specific entries ... */
    }
    route-filter-list RoutesToExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        ::/0 prefix-length-range /1-/127;
        $PREFIX6/$LEN6 upto /128;
        /* ... additional site-specific entries ... */
    }
}

snips.name: vrf-evpn-ip-prefix
snips.topic: VRF instance with EVPN ip-prefix-routes, optional eBGP external peering, and DHCP relay
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the Junos sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — VRF with EVPN Type-5 ip-prefix-routes.
snips.bodyHtml:
routing-instances {
    $VRF_NAME {
        instance-type vrf;
        routing-options {
            rib $VRF_NAME.inet6.0 {
                multipath;
            }
            graceful-restart;
            multipath;
            auto-export;
        }
        protocols {
            bgp {
                group l3rtr {
                    type external;
                    multihop {
                        ttl 1;
                    }
                    family inet {
                        unicast {
                            loops 2;
                        }
                    }
                    family inet6 {
                        unicast {
                            loops 2;
                        }
                    }
                    multipath {
                        multiple-as;
                    }
                    neighbor $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V6 {
                        description facing_$EXT_PEER_NAME;
                        multihop {
                            ttl 2;
                        }
                        local-address $EXT_LOCAL_V6;
                        import ( RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        family inet6 {
                            unicast;
                        }
                        export ( RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        peer-as $EXT_PEER_AS;
                    }
                    neighbor $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V4 {
                        description facing_$EXT_PEER_NAME;
                        multihop {
                            ttl 2;
                        }
                        local-address $EXT_LOCAL_V4;
                        import ( RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        family inet {
                            unicast;
                        }
                        export ( RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        peer-as $EXT_PEER_AS;
                    }
                }
                graceful-restart {
                    dont-help-shared-fate-bfd-down;
                }
            }
            evpn {
                ip-prefix-routes {
                    advertise direct-nexthop;
                    encapsulation vxlan;
                    vni $VNI;
                    export BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME;
                }
            }
        }
        forwarding-options {
            dhcp-relay {
                forward-only;
                server-group {
                    $VRF_NAME {
                        $DHCP_SERVER;
                    }
                }
                group $VRF_NAME {
                    active-server-group $VRF_NAME;
                    relay-option-82 {
                        server-id-override;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        interface $WAN_INTERFACE;
        interface lo0.$LO_UNIT;
        route-distinguisher $RD;
        vrf-target $VRF_TARGET;
    }
}

snips.name: bgp-evpn-overlay
snips.topic: eBGP EVPN overlay peer group with multihop and signaling
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — group name "l3clos-l-evpn" (leaf) or "l3clos-s-evpn" (spine).
Overlay eBGP sessions use loopback addresses (multihop ttl 1) — decoupled from physical link state, survives individual link failures.
no-nexthop-change preserves the originating VTEP address through the spine.
family evpn signaling with loops 2 allows type-5 routes to re-enter the fabric for inter-VRF routing via border leaf.
BFD at 3000ms×3 (slower than underlay — overlay is multihop).
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    bgp {
        group $EVPN_GROUP {
            type external;
            multihop {
                ttl 1;
                no-nexthop-change;
            }
            family evpn {
                signaling {
                    loops 2;
                }
            }
            multipath {
                multiple-as;
            }
            bfd-liveness-detection {
                minimum-interval $BFD_INTERVAL_MS;
                multiplier $BFD_MULTIPLIER;
            }
            neighbor $OVERLAY_PEER {
                description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
                local-address $OVERLAY_LOCAL;
                family evpn {
                    signaling;
                }
                export ( LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT && EVPN_EXPORT );
                peer-as $PEER_AS;
            }
            vpn-apply-export;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: bgp-underlay-fabric
snips.topic: eBGP underlay fabric peer group (leaf-to-spine or spine-to-leaf)
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — group naming convention: "l3clos-s" for spine, "l3clos-l" for leaf. This template shows the leaf-to-spine variant.
eBGP with unique ASN per device (Apstra default: spine 64512/64513, leaf 64514–64518) — enables ECMP multipath with multiple-as.
BFD liveness at 1000ms×3 for sub-second failure detection on fabric links.
vpn-apply-export ensures VPN routes (EVPN) inherit the fabric export policy.
Spine variant adds SPINE_TO_LEAF_FABRIC_OUT export; leaf adds LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT.

pairWith.raw: evo/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf         (overlay BGP uses same spine peers)
pairWith.note: overlay BGP uses same spine peers

pairWith.raw: evo/interfaces/fabric-uplink.conf            (physical links these sessions run over)
pairWith.note: physical links these sessions run over

snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    bgp {
        group $BGP_GROUP {
            type external;
            multipath {
                multiple-as;
            }
            bfd-liveness-detection {
                minimum-interval $BFD_INTERVAL_MS;
                multiplier $BFD_MULTIPLIER;
            }
            neighbor $PEER_IP {
                description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
                local-address $LOCAL_IP;
                family inet {
                    unicast;
                }
                export ( LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT && BGP-AOS-Policy );
                peer-as $PEER_AS;
            }
            vpn-apply-export;
        }
        log-updown;
        graceful-restart {
            dont-help-shared-fate-bfd-down;
        }
        multipath;
    }
}

snips.name: forwarding-table-ecmp
snips.topic: Forwarding-table ECMP with per-packet load-balance and chained-composite-next-hop
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — standard ECMP tuning for EVPN/VXLAN fabrics.
export PFE-LB: applies the per-packet load-balance policy to all forwarding lookups.
ecmp-fast-reroute: pre-programs backup ECMP paths for sub-second convergence when a spine link fails.

chained-composite-next-hop ingress evpn: required on leaf/border-leaf for EVPN Type-5 symmetric routing — chains the VXLAN encap with the VRF lookup. Spine omits this block (no EVPN data-plane termination).
snips.bodyHtml:
routing-options {
    router-id $ROUTER_ID;
    autonomous-system $LOCAL_AS;
    graceful-restart;
    forwarding-table {
        export PFE-LB;
        ecmp-fast-reroute;
        chained-composite-next-hop {
            ingress {
                evpn;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: chassis-port-config
snips.topic: Chassis aggregated-devices and FPC port-speed configuration for QFX5120 leaf
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — device-count matches the number of LAG bundles provisioned by the Apstra intent model.
aggregated-devices ethernet device-count must be >= the highest aeN unit number in use (ae1, ae2 → device-count 2).
Uplink ports (et-0/0/48, et-0/0/49) are set to 100g for spine connectivity; access ports are 10g by default on QFX5120-48Y.

pairWith.raw: junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf  (LAGs that consume the aggregated-devices count)
pairWith.note: LAGs that consume the aggregated-devices count

snips.bodyHtml:
chassis {
    aggregated-devices {
        ethernet {
            device-count $LAG_DEVICE_COUNT;
        }
    }
    fpc 0 {
        pic 0 {
            port $UPLINK_PORT_1 {
                speed $UPLINK_SPEED;
            }
            port $UPLINK_PORT_2 {
                speed $UPLINK_SPEED;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: grpc-certificate
snips.topic: gRPC TLS certificate for Juniper Apstra controller authentication
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — the certificate name "aos_grpc" is the Apstra convention (AOS = Apstra Operating System, the former product name).
SECRET-DATA placeholder replaces the actual PEM-encoded cert+key at commit time.
snips.bodyHtml:
security {
    certificates {
        local {
            $CERT_NAME {
                "$SECRET_DATA"; ## SECRET-DATA
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: external-vlan-tagged
snips.topic: Flexible-VLAN-tagged external interface for inter-VRF/WAN exit
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — Junos QFX5120-32C variant uses xe-0/0/0 as the external-facing port (no breakout needed, native 10G).
flexible-vlan-tagging with per-VRF VLAN units (same logical pattern as EVO sibling).

Includes EVPN-VXLAN forwarding-options (shared-tunnels, vxlan-routing) that are configured at the top level on Junos — not present on EVO border leafs which don't terminate VXLAN tunnels for fabric VLANs.

pairWith.raw: junos/interfaces/irb-gateway.conf          (IRB pattern for VRF L3 exit)
pairWith.note: IRB pattern for VRF L3 exit

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $EXT_INTF {
        flexible-vlan-tagging;
        native-vlan-id $NATIVE_VLAN;
    }
}

snips.name: fabric-uplink
snips.topic: Point-to-point fabric uplink to spine with /31 IPv4 addressing
highlights: Body is structurally identical to the EVO sibling (no "speed" knob on Junos QFX5120 — speed is set in chassis fpc/pic/port stanza instead).
Generated by Juniper Apstra — description follows "facing_<peer>:<port>" convention.
MTU 9216 at physical layer, 9170 at IP layer (46 bytes for L2 overhead).
QFX5120-48Y uses et-0/0/48, et-0/0/49 for 100G spine uplinks. QFX5120-32C uses et-0/0/48 for spine uplinks.

pairWith.raw: junos/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf  (BGP sessions run over these links)
pairWith.note: BGP sessions run over these links

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $FABRIC_INTF {
        description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
        mtu $LINK_MTU;
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                mtu $IP_MTU;
                address $LOCAL_IP_SLASH31;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: irb-gateway
snips.topic: IRB gateway interface for EVPN/VXLAN symmetric routing (ERB model)
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — one IRB unit per VLAN/VNI in the Edge-Routed Bridging (ERB) overlay; this snip shows the repeating per-VLAN pattern.
Dual-stack (inet + inet6) with 9000-byte MTU for jumbo frame support.
Static MAC (00:1c:73:00:00:01) shared across all leaf IRBs in the same VRF — this is the Apstra "anycast gateway" MAC enabling seamless VM mobility.
no-dhcp-flood on the first IRB unit (DHCP relay active on that subnet).
In practice hundreds of these units exist; template one and repeat per VLAN.

pairWith.raw: junos/interfaces/vlan-vxlan-domain.conf  (vlans block maps VLAN to VNI + l3-interface)
pairWith.note: vlans block maps VLAN to VNI + l3-interface

pairWith.raw: junos/oam/ipv6-router-advertisement.conf (IPv6 RA on each IRB unit)
pairWith.note: IPv6 RA on each IRB unit

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    irb {
        unit $IRB_UNIT {
            family inet {
                mtu $IRB_MTU;
                address $IRB_IPV4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                mtu $IRB_MTU;
                address $IRB_IPV6;
            }
            mac $ANYCAST_MAC;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: lag-esi-access
snips.topic: LAG with EVPN ESI all-active multihoming for redundant server access
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — ESI value and LACP system-id follow Apstra's deterministic allocation scheme.
ESI Type 0 (manually configured) with all-active load-balancing across the ESI leaf pair — both leafs forward traffic for the same MAC.
LACP system-id is shared between the two ESI peers so the server sees a single logical LAG partner.
Trunk mode carries all VLAN members mapped to the tenant VNIs.
MTU 9216 for jumbo frame passthrough.
snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $AE_INTF {
        description $AE_DESCRIPTION;
        mtu $AE_MTU;
        esi {
            $ESI_VALUE;
            all-active;
        }
        aggregated-ether-options {
            lacp {
                active;
                system-id $LACP_SYSTEM_ID;
            }
        }
        unit 0 {
            family ethernet-switching {
                interface-mode trunk;
                vlan {
                    members $VLAN_MEMBERS;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: loopback-multi-unit
snips.topic: Multi-unit loopback for router-id, EVPN overlay, and per-VRF identities
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — unit allocation follows Apstra ID assignment.
Unit 0: router-id + underlay reachability (BGP overlay local-address source).
Units 2+: per-VRF loopback for route-distinguisher (one per tenant VRF).
Leaf nodes typically have 3–4 units (red, blue, green VRFs).

pairWith.raw: junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf (VRF references lo0 unit for RD)
pairWith.note: VRF references lo0 unit for RD

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    lo0 {
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                address $ROUTER_ID_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $ROUTER_ID_V6;
            }
        }
        unit $VRF_UNIT {
            family inet {
                address $VRF_LO_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $VRF_LO_V6;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: trunk-access-port
snips.topic: L2 trunk access port carrying EVPN/VXLAN VLANs to servers
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — VLAN members list is auto-populated from the Apstra connectivity template assigned to the server-facing port.
Single-homed access: no ESI, no LAG — used for standalone server connections.
MTU 9216 allows jumbo frames end-to-end within the VXLAN fabric.
In this JVD, the VLAN members list is large (~600 VLANs per VRF); template shows the pattern, not the full enumeration.
snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $ACCESS_INTF {
        description $ACCESS_DESC;
        mtu $ACCESS_MTU;
        unit 0 {
            family ethernet-switching {
                interface-mode trunk;
                vlan {
                    members $VLAN_MEMBERS;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: vlan-vxlan-domain
snips.topic: VLAN-to-VXLAN domain mapping with VNI and optional L3 IRB interface
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — VLAN names follow Apstra "vn<id>" convention with descriptions indicating VRF + subnet purpose (e.g. "red_all_400").
This JVD has ~600 VLANs per VRF; this template shows the repeating pattern.
l3-interface irb.<unit> enables symmetric IRB routing in the ERB model — present only on VLANs that need L3 gateway (routed VRFs).
VLANs without l3-interface are pure L2 segments (green VRF in this JVD).
Defined inside the mac-vrf routing instance's vlans block on Junos.

pairWith.raw: junos/services/mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan.conf  (mac-vrf instance that owns these vlans)
pairWith.note: mac-vrf instance that owns these vlans

snips.bodyHtml:
vlans {
    $VLAN_NAME {
        description $VLAN_DESC;
        vlan-id $VLAN_ID;
        l3-interface $L3_INTERFACE;
        vxlan {
            vni $VNI_ID;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: ipv6-router-advertisement
snips.topic: IPv6 Router Advertisement on IRB/WAN interfaces
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — enables SLAAC/RA on external-facing interfaces for IPv6 neighbor discovery and prefix advertisement.
Tuned intervals (max 30s / min 10s) provide faster convergence than defaults.
Applied to WAN-facing VLAN sub-interfaces on border-leaf only.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    router-advertisement {
        interface $INTERFACE {
            max-advertisement-interval 30;
            min-advertisement-interval 10;
        }
        /* ... repeat per IPv6-enabled external interface ... */
    }
}

snips.name: l2-learning-telemetry
snips.topic: L2 learning telemetry for remote MAC entry visibility
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — enables streaming of remote (EVPN-learned) MAC table entries to telemetry collectors for fabric-wide MAC visibility.
Single knob; no additional parameters required.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    l2-learning {
        telemetry {
            enable-remote-entries;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: lldp-discovery
snips.topic: LLDP neighbor discovery configuration
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — enables LLDP on all interfaces for topology discovery and Apstra device-lifecycle management.
port-id-subtype interface-name ensures LLDP TLVs carry the logical interface name (not MAC), matching Apstra's wiring validation expectations.
neighbour-port-info-display port-id controls how neighbor info appears in "show lldp neighbors" output.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    lldp {
        port-id-subtype interface-name;
        port-description-type interface-description;
        neighbour-port-info-display port-id;
        interface all;
    }
}

snips.name: rstp-bridge
snips.topic: RSTP bridge configuration with edge-port BPDU blocking
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — leaf/border-leaf runs RSTP as root bridge (bridge-priority 0) to maintain a predictable L2 topology.
bpdu-block-on-edge disables ports receiving unexpected BPDUs (server-facing ports should never send BPDUs; if they do, it indicates a loop or miscabling).
Spine has `protocols rstp { disable; }` (no L2 on spine in pure L3 CLOS).

pairWith.raw: junos/interfaces/lag-esi-access.conf   (edge interfaces protected by BPDU block)
pairWith.note: edge interfaces protected by BPDU block

snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    rstp {
        bridge-priority 0;
        bpdu-block-on-edge;
    }
}

snips.name: sflow-telemetry
snips.topic: sFlow telemetry sampling and collector configuration
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — sFlow provides per-interface packet sampling for traffic visibility and capacity planning.
Sample-rate 1:128 ingress/egress with 20s polling balances telemetry granularity against CPU overhead.
source-ip should be a loopback or management IP reachable from the collector.
Interfaces list is typically all fabric-facing and access-facing ports.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    sflow {
        polling-interval 20;
        sample-rate {
            ingress 128;
            egress 128;
        }
        source-ip $SOURCE_IP;
        collector $COLLECTOR_IP {
            udp-port $COLLECTOR_PORT;
        }
        interfaces $INTERFACE;
        /* ... repeat per monitored interface ... */
    }
}

snips.name: bgp-aos-export-policy
snips.topic: BGP-AOS-Policy master export filter chaining AllPodNetworks and BGP redistribution
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — "BGP-AOS-Policy" is the Apstra master export policy applied to all fabric BGP sessions (both underlay and per-VRF).
Base policy accepts routes matching AllPodNetworks OR received via BGP; rejects all else.
Per-VRF variants (BGP-AOS-Policy-blue, -red) chain to AllPodNetworks-<vrf> and are applied to the VRF's EVPN ip-prefix-routes export.
Spine has only the base; leaf/border-leaf adds per-VRF variants.

pairWith.raw: junos/transport/bgp-underlay-fabric.conf  (fabric BGP applies this as export)
pairWith.note: fabric BGP applies this as export

snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-20 {
            from protocol bgp;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-20 {
            from protocol bgp;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: community-definitions
snips.topic: BGP community definitions for fabric signaling and route tagging
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — community values encode fabric-tier origin, address-family, and VRF membership for route-leak and loop-prevention policies.
FABRIC_COMMUNITIES uses a wildcard regex match to bulk-delete all internal communities when exporting to external peers.
Spine devices carry only DEFAULT_DIRECT, FROM_SPINE_*, and FABRIC_COMMUNITIES.
Leaf/border-leaf adds per-VRF and RoutesFromExt communities.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    /* Base communities — present on all devices */
    community DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4 members [ 3:20007 21001:26000 ];
    community DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6 members [ 3:20008 21001:26000 ];
    community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER members 0:14;
    community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER members 0:15;
    community FABRIC_COMMUNITIES members [ 0:12 0:13 0:14 0:15 .+:200.. 2....:260.. ];

    /* Per-VRF communities — one pair (v4 + v6) per VRF on leaf/border-leaf */
    community $VRF_COMM_V4 members $VRF_MEMBERS_V4;
    community $VRF_COMM_V6 members $VRF_MEMBERS_V6;

    /* External-peer import communities — border-leaf only */
    community RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME members [ 3:20009 $VRF_ID:26000 ];
    community RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME members [ 3:20010 $VRF_ID:26000 ];
}

snips.name: external-route-export
snips.topic: External route export policy with fabric community stripping
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — applied as BGP export toward external peers.
Strips internal fabric communities (FABRIC_COMMUNITIES) before advertising routes externally — prevents internal topology leakage.
Uses route-filter-lists to control exactly which prefixes are exported per VRF.
Dual-stack: separate inet/inet6 terms with corresponding filter-lists.

pairWith.raw: junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf     (VRF BGP applies this as export)
pairWith.note: VRF BGP applies this as export

snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                route-filter-list RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community delete FABRIC_COMMUNITIES;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                route-filter-list RoutesToExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community delete FABRIC_COMMUNITIES;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-30 {
            from family inet;
            then reject;
        }
        term RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-40 {
            from family inet6;
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: external-route-import
snips.topic: External route import policy with route-filter-list and community tagging
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — applied as BGP import on external VRF peers.
Dual-stack: separate terms for inet (v4) and inet6 (v6) with corresponding route-filter-lists and community values.
Routes matching the filter-list are tagged with a per-VRF import community for fabric-wide route identification.
Final reject terms ensure only explicitly permitted prefixes enter the VRF.

pairWith.raw: junos/services/vrf-evpn-ip-prefix.conf     (VRF BGP applies this as import)
pairWith.note: VRF BGP applies this as import

snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                route-filter-list RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community add $IMPORT_COMMUNITY;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                route-filter-list RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME;
            }
            then {
                community add $IMPORT_COMM_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-30 {
            from family inet;
            then reject;
        }
        term RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME-40 {
            from family inet6;
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: leaf-to-spine-evpn-filter
snips.topic: LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT loop-prevention filter rejecting spine-originated EVPN routes
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — prevents EVPN routes learned from the spine tier from being re-advertised back to spines (loop prevention in eBGP CLOS).
Matches community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER (0:14) set by the spine's SPINE_TO_LEAF_EVPN_OUT export policy.
Applied as export on the leaf's l3clos-l-evpn BGP group toward spines.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT {
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT-10 {
            from {
                protocol bgp;
                community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER;
            }
            then reject;
        }
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT-20 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: leaf-to-spine-fabric-filter
snips.topic: LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT loop-prevention filter rejecting spine-originated fabric routes
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — prevents IPv4 unicast fabric routes learned from spines from being re-advertised back (underlay loop prevention).
Matches community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER (0:15) set by spine's SPINE_TO_LEAF_FABRIC_OUT export.
Applied as export on the leaf's l3clos-l BGP group toward spines.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT {
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT-10 {
            from {
                protocol bgp;
                community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER;
            }
            then reject;
        }
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT-20 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: per-packet-load-balance
snips.topic: Per-packet load-balance policy for PFE forwarding-table export
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — identical on all devices regardless of role or OS.
"per-packet" is a misnomer carried from early Junos — it actually means per-flow hash-based ECMP (not true per-packet round-robin).
Referenced by routing-options forwarding-table export on every device.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement PFE-LB {
        then {
            load-balance per-packet;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: pod-network-export
snips.topic: AllPodNetworks direct-route export policy with per-VRF community tagging
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — "AllPodNetworks" is the Apstra convention for tagging directly-connected subnets with community values for fabric-wide filtering.
Base policy (AllPodNetworks) tags all direct routes with DEFAULT_DIRECT communities.
Per-VRF variants (AllPodNetworks-blue, AllPodNetworks-red) add VRF-specific community values — used by EVPN ip-prefix-routes export on leaf nodes.
Spine has only the base policy; leafs/border-leafs add per-VRF variants.
Term ordering: inet (v4) → inet6 (v6) → reject-all (safety net).
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks {
        term AllPodNetworks-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME {
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $VRF_COMMUNITY_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $VRF_COMMUNITY_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-$VRF_NAME-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: route-filter-lists
snips.topic: Route-filter-list definitions for external peer import/export control
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — one set of filter-lists per VRF per external peer.
RoutesFromExt: simple default-route match (0.0.0.0/0 exact) — accepts only a default from the external peer.
RoutesToExt: detailed prefix ranges defining which internal subnets are advertised externally; values are site-specific.
IPv6 variants mirror the IPv4 logic with 2001:db8: documentation prefixes.
Border-leaf only; leaf/spine devices have no route-filter-lists.
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    /* Import filter — accept only default route from external peer */
    route-filter-list RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0.0.0.0/0 exact;
    }
    route-filter-list RoutesFromExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0::0/0 exact;
    }

    /* Export filter — advertise specific internal prefixes to external peer */
    route-filter-list RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        0.0.0.0/0 prefix-length-range /1-/32;
        $PREFIX/$LEN upto /32;
        /* ... additional site-specific entries ... */
    }
    route-filter-list RoutesToExtV6-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME {
        ::/0 prefix-length-range /1-/127;
        $PREFIX6/$LEN6 upto /128;
        /* ... additional site-specific entries ... */
    }
}

snips.name: mac-vrf-evpn-vxlan
snips.topic: MAC-VRF instance with EVPN/VXLAN encapsulation and per-VNI route-targets
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — service-type vlan-aware mac-vrf aggregates all L2 VNIs into a single EVPN instance with per-VNI vrf-target for fine-grained import/export control.
default-gateway do-not-advertise suppresses Type-2 MAC/IP for anycast gateways (expected with ERB — each leaf owns its own gateway MAC locally).
duplicate-mac-detection with auto-recovery-time 9 handles loop/miscabling.
extended-vni-list "all" enables all VNIs without explicit enumeration on the forwarding plane.
vlans stanza maps VLAN-ID → VNI and binds the L3 IRB interface for routed traffic.
Junos-only pattern — EVO border-leaf devices use a different EVPN model.
snips.bodyHtml:
routing-instances {
    $INSTANCE_NAME {
        instance-type mac-vrf;
        protocols {
            evpn {
                encapsulation vxlan;
                default-gateway do-not-advertise;
                duplicate-mac-detection {
                    auto-recovery-time $AUTO_RECOVERY_TIME;
                }
                extended-vni-list all;
                vni-options {
                    vni $VNI {
                        vrf-target $VNI_TARGET;
                    }
                    /* ... repeat per VNI ... */
                }
            }
        }
        vtep-source-interface $VTEP_SOURCE;
        service-type vlan-aware;
        interface $ACCESS_INTERFACE;
        /* ... repeat per access interface ... */
        route-distinguisher $RD;
        vrf-target $GLOBAL_VRF_TARGET;
        vlans {
            $VLAN_NAME {
                description $VLAN_DESC;
                vlan-id $VLAN_ID;
                l3-interface irb.$IRB_UNIT;
                vxlan {
                    vni $VXLAN_VNI;
                }
            }
            /* ... repeat per VLAN ... */
        }
    }
}

snips.name: vrf-evpn-ip-prefix
snips.topic: VRF instance with EVPN ip-prefix-routes, optional eBGP external peering, and DHCP relay
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — instance-type vrf with EVPN Type-5 ip-prefix-routes.
Border-leaf variant includes protocols bgp group "l3rtr" for eBGP peering to external routers (dual-stack, import/export policies per peer).
Leaf variant omits BGP — only EVPN + DHCP relay + interface list.
DHCP relay uses forward-only with relay-option-82 server-id-override.
route-distinguisher and vrf-target are auto-assigned by Apstra.
snips.bodyHtml:
routing-instances {
    $VRF_NAME {
        instance-type vrf;
        routing-options {
            rib $VRF_NAME.inet6.0 {
                multipath;
            }
            graceful-restart;
            multipath;
            auto-export;
        }
        protocols {
            /* Border-leaf only: eBGP to external router */
            bgp {
                group l3rtr {
                    type external;
                    multihop {
                        ttl 1;
                    }
                    family inet {
                        unicast {
                            loops 2;
                        }
                    }
                    family inet6 {
                        unicast {
                            loops 2;
                        }
                    }
                    multipath {
                        multiple-as;
                    }
                    neighbor $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V6 {
                        description facing_$EXT_PEER_NAME;
                        multihop {
                            ttl 2;
                        }
                        local-address $EXT_LOCAL_V6;
                        import ( RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        family inet6 {
                            unicast;
                        }
                        export ( RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        peer-as $EXT_PEER_AS;
                    }
                    neighbor $EXT_NEIGHBOR_V4 {
                        description facing_$EXT_PEER_NAME;
                        multihop {
                            ttl 2;
                        }
                        local-address $EXT_LOCAL_V4;
                        import ( RoutesFromExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        family inet {
                            unicast;
                        }
                        export ( RoutesToExt-$VRF_NAME-$EXT_PEER_NAME );
                        peer-as $EXT_PEER_AS;
                    }
                }
                graceful-restart {
                    dont-help-shared-fate-bfd-down;
                }
            }
            evpn {
                ip-prefix-routes {
                    advertise direct-nexthop;
                    encapsulation vxlan;
                    vni $VNI;
                    export BGP-AOS-Policy-$VRF_NAME;
                }
            }
        }
        forwarding-options {
            dhcp-relay {
                forward-only;
                server-group {
                    $VRF_NAME {
                        $DHCP_SERVER;
                    }
                }
                group $VRF_NAME {
                    active-server-group $VRF_NAME;
                    relay-option-82 {
                        server-id-override;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        interface $WAN_INTERFACE;
        interface lo0.$LO_UNIT;
        route-distinguisher $RD;
        vrf-target $VRF_TARGET;
    }
}

snips.name: bgp-evpn-overlay
snips.topic: eBGP EVPN overlay peer group with multihop and signaling
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — group name "l3clos-l-evpn" for leaf.
Overlay eBGP sessions use loopback addresses (multihop ttl 1), decoupled from physical link state.
no-nexthop-change preserves originating VTEP address through spine.
family evpn signaling loops 2 for inter-VRF re-entry via border leaf.
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    bgp {
        group $EVPN_GROUP {
            type external;
            multihop {
                ttl 1;
                no-nexthop-change;
            }
            family evpn {
                signaling {
                    loops 2;
                }
            }
            multipath {
                multiple-as;
            }
            bfd-liveness-detection {
                minimum-interval $BFD_INTERVAL_MS;
                multiplier $BFD_MULTIPLIER;
            }
            neighbor $OVERLAY_PEER {
                description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
                local-address $OVERLAY_LOCAL;
                family evpn {
                    signaling;
                }
                export ( LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT && EVPN_EXPORT );
                peer-as $PEER_AS;
            }
            vpn-apply-export;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: bgp-underlay-fabric
snips.topic: eBGP underlay fabric peer group (leaf-to-spine or spine-to-leaf)
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — group name "l3clos-l" for leaf, "l3clos-s" for spine.
eBGP with unique ASN per device; multipath multiple-as enables ECMP across spines.
BFD at 1000ms×3 for sub-second fabric convergence.
vpn-apply-export pushes EVPN routes through the fabric export chain.

pairWith.raw: junos/transport/bgp-evpn-overlay.conf         (overlay BGP uses same spine peers)
pairWith.note: overlay BGP uses same spine peers

snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    bgp {
        group $BGP_GROUP {
            type external;
            multipath {
                multiple-as;
            }
            bfd-liveness-detection {
                minimum-interval $BFD_INTERVAL_MS;
                multiplier $BFD_MULTIPLIER;
            }
            neighbor $PEER_IP {
                description $PEER_DESCRIPTION;
                local-address $LOCAL_IP;
                family inet {
                    unicast;
                }
                export ( LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT && BGP-AOS-Policy );
                peer-as $PEER_AS;
            }
            vpn-apply-export;
        }
        log-updown;
        graceful-restart {
            dont-help-shared-fate-bfd-down;
        }
        multipath;
    }
}

snips.name: evpn-vxlan-shared-tunnels
snips.topic: EVPN-VXLAN shared tunnels and VXLAN routing parameters
highlights: Generated by Juniper Apstra — these forwarding-options are Junos-specific; EVO platforms handle VXLAN tunnel sharing implicitly.
shared-tunnels: multiple VNIs share the same VXLAN tunnel to each remote VTEP, reducing the number of next-hops in the forwarding table.
vxlan-routing next-hop 45056: pre-allocates next-hop table entries for symmetric IRB routing across VNIs.
interface-num 8192: maximum number of VXLAN logical interfaces (scales with VNI count).
overlay-ecmp: enables ECMP across multiple VXLAN paths to the same destination.
snips.bodyHtml:
forwarding-options {
    evpn-vxlan {
        shared-tunnels;
    }
    vxlan-routing {
        next-hop $VXLAN_NEXT_HOP;
        interface-num $VXLAN_INTF_NUM;
        overlay-ecmp;
    }
}

snips.name: forwarding-table-ecmp
snips.topic: Forwarding-table ECMP with per-packet load-balance and chained-composite-next-hop
highlights: Body is byte-identical to the EVO sibling.
Generated by Juniper Apstra — standard ECMP tuning for EVPN/VXLAN fabrics.
export PFE-LB applies per-packet load-balance to all forwarding lookups.
ecmp-fast-reroute pre-programs backup paths for sub-second spine failover.
chained-composite-next-hop ingress evpn: required for EVPN Type-5 symmetric routing on leaf/border-leaf nodes.
snips.bodyHtml:
routing-options {
    router-id $ROUTER_ID;
    autonomous-system $LOCAL_AS;
    graceful-restart;
    forwarding-table {
        export PFE-LB;
        ecmp-fast-reroute;
        chained-composite-next-hop {
            ingress {
                evpn;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: chassis-buffer-monitor
snips.topic: FPC traffic-manager buffer-monitor for AI/RoCE fabric visibility
highlights: Enables per-FPC buffer-occupancy telemetry on QFX5240 — required to observe PFC pause headroom and ECN marking thresholds in real time
Necessary for RoCEv2 lossless fabric tuning; pairs with the CoS shared-buffer / lossless-headroom partition definitions
Single-FPC pizza-box; replicate the `fpc N` block per slot on chassis
snips.bodyHtml:
chassis {
    fpc 0 {
        traffic-manager {
            buffer-monitor-enable;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: security-grpc-cert
snips.topic: Local TLS certificate for the gRPC extension-service listener
highlights: Holds the `aos_grpc` cert/key pair referenced by the gRPC SSL stanza
The actual key material is masked as "secret" ## SECRET-DATA — the template only carries the structure; supply real PEM at deploy time
Cert name is fixed (`aos_grpc`) because the system extension-service block references it by literal name
snips.bodyHtml:
security {
    certificates {
        local {
            aos_grpc {
                "$GRPC_CERT_PEM"; ## SECRET-DATA
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: system-grpc-netconf
snips.topic: System base — netconf/SSH, gRPC extension-service over TLS, mgmt instance
highlights: gRPC request-response listener on port 32767 with TLS, anchored to the dedicated mgmt_junos routing-instance — keeps Apstra / streaming telemetry off the in-band fabric
`management-instance` puts the management ethernet in mgmt_junos so OOB and in-band have separate route tables (no leakage)
ddos-protection `resolve aggregate disable-fpc` prevents PFC pause storms from reaching the RE during heavy GPU-fabric incast
Pairs with security/certificates for the `aos_grpc` local-cert
snips.bodyHtml:
system {
    host-name $HOSTNAME;
    services {
        netconf {
            ssh;
        }
        extension-service {
            request-response {
                grpc {
                    ssl {
                        port $GRPC_PORT;
                        local-certificate aos_grpc;
                    }
                    routing-instance mgmt_junos;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    management-instance;
    ddos-protection {
        protocols {
            resolve {
                aggregate {
                    disable-fpc;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: rdma-rocev2-lossless
snips.topic: RoCEv2 lossless CoS — DSCP classifier, PFC, ECN, lossless buffers, schedulers
highlights: Two forwarding-classes carry the GPU traffic: NO-LOSS  (queue 4, lossless, PFC priority 3, ECN-marked via WRED) CNP      (queue 3, strict-high, 5% rate cap — for RoCEv2 Congestion Notification Packets so they bypass the very congestion they're reporting)
DSCP classifier `mydscp` maps DSCP 011010 (CS3 = 26) -> NO-LOSS and DSCP 110000 (CS6 = 48) -> CNP, matching standard RoCEv2 marking
Drop profile dp1 starts marking ECN at 55% buffer fill, 100% at 90% — the classic RoCEv2 ECN profile recommended by NVIDIA / DCQCN

shared-buffer ingress: 66% lossless + 24% lossless-headroom (the PFC pause headroom) + 10% lossy. egress: 66% lossless + 10% lossy. The headroom is sized for cable length + PFC reaction time on a single-tier fabric; revisit for multi-tier or longer cabling.
dedicated-buffer ingress 15% / egress 30% — per-port reservation that isolates one congested port from starving its neighbors
`pfc-watchdog` auto-restores a queue stuck in PFC-paused state
The `interfaces et-* unit *` wildcard applies the classifier and scheduler-map to every et-* port automatically — no per-port repetition
snips.bodyHtml:
class-of-service {
    classifiers {
        dscp mydscp {
            forwarding-class CNP {
                loss-priority low code-points 110000;
            }
            forwarding-class NO-LOSS {
                loss-priority low code-points 011010;
            }
        }
    }
    drop-profiles {
        dp1 {
            interpolate {
                fill-level [ 55 90 ];
                drop-probability [ 0 100 ];
            }
        }
    }
    shared-buffer {
        ingress {
            buffer-partition lossless {
                percent 66;
                dynamic-threshold 10;
            }
            buffer-partition lossless-headroom {
                percent 24;
            }
            buffer-partition lossy {
                percent 10;
            }
        }
        egress {
            buffer-partition lossless {
                percent 66;
            }
            buffer-partition lossy {
                percent 10;
            }
        }
    }
    dedicated-buffer {
        ingress {
            percent 15;
        }
        egress {
            percent 30;
        }
    }
    forwarding-classes {
        class CNP queue-num 3;
        class NO-LOSS queue-num 4 no-loss pfc-priority 3;
    }
    congestion-notification-profile {
        cnp {
            pfc-watchdog;
            input {
                dscp {
                    code-point 011010 {
                        pfc;
                    }
                }
            }
            output {
                ieee-802.1 {
                    code-point 011 {
                        flow-control-queue 4;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
    interfaces {
        et-* {
            congestion-notification-profile cnp;
            scheduler-map sm1;
            unit * {
                classifiers {
                    dscp mydscp;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    scheduler-maps {
        sm1 {
            forwarding-class CNP scheduler s2-cnp;
            forwarding-class NO-LOSS scheduler s1;
        }
    }
    schedulers {
        s1 {
            drop-profile-map loss-priority any protocol any drop-profile dp1;
            explicit-congestion-notification;
        }
        s2-cnp {
            transmit-rate percent 5;
            priority strict-high;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: fabric-p2p-400g-breakout
snips.topic: 400G breakout to 2x sub-ports for fabric P2P /31 links (MTU 9216/9170)
highlights: Two-step pattern: parent port declares `number-of-sub-ports 2` + `speed 400g`, then each `:N` sub-port carries the actual L3 config
Physical MTU 9216 / IP MTU 9170 — leaves 46B headroom for VXLAN + underlay encap so encapsulated 9000B tenant frames don't fragment
/31 numbering on every spine<->leaf link; underlay is pure IPv4 eBGP unnumbered-style with explicit local-address per session
No `family inet6` on fabric links (IPv6 is tenant-side only)
snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $PHY_PORT {
        description "Breakout $PHY_PORT";
        number-of-sub-ports 2;
        speed 400g;
    }
    ${PHY_PORT}:0 {
        description facing_$PEER_HOST:$PEER_PORT_A;
        mtu 9216;
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                mtu 9170;
                address $LOCAL_V4_A;
            }
        }
    }
    ${PHY_PORT}:1 {
        description facing_$PEER_HOST:$PEER_PORT_B;
        mtu 9216;
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                mtu 9170;
                address $LOCAL_V4_B;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: irb-tenant-gateway
snips.topic: IRB anycast gateway per tenant — MTU 9000, shared virtual MAC
highlights: One IRB unit per tenant subnet; the SAME virtual MAC (00:1c:73:00:00:01) is configured on every leaf so any leaf can answer ARP for the tenant gateway (anycast-gateway pattern, no VRRP needed)
IRB MTU 9000 (jumbo) sized for AI/ML payloads; underlying physical interfaces use MTU 9216 to leave headroom for VXLAN encapsulation
Each unit's family inet address is the tenant subnet's gateway IP
Unit numbers are globally meaningful — pick a per-tenant unit-number convention and stick to it across the fabric

pairWith.note: VRFs that bind these IRB units

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    irb {
        mtu 9216;
        unit $IRB_UNIT {
            family inet {
                mtu 9000;
                address $TENANT_GW_V4;
            }
            mac $ANYCAST_MAC;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: loopback-leaf
snips.topic: Leaf loopback — primary unit (underlay/overlay) + per-VRF units
highlights: unit 0 is the device's underlay router-id and the EVPN overlay eBGP local-address — single loopback drives both planes
Each tenant VRF gets its own lo0.N unit, bound into the VRF's interface list so the EVPN type-5 (ip-prefix-routes) RIB has a next-hop anchor
Dual-stack v4 + v6 on every unit; v6 ULA range fdf6:ed70:1fac:f2d2::/64
The unit-number-to-VRF mapping must match the IRB unit-number convention (see irb-tenant-gateway snip)
snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    lo0 {
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                address $LO0_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $LO0_V6;
            }
        }
        unit $TENANT_UNIT {
            family inet {
                address $TENANT_LO_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $TENANT_LO_V6;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: loopback-spine
snips.topic: Spine loopback — single unit serving both underlay router-id and EVPN overlay
highlights: Spines have NO tenant VRFs (pure underlay + EVPN route-relay), so a single lo0.0 covers both the underlay router-id and the eBGP-EVPN overlay local-address
Dual-stack v4 + v6 (v6 ULA fdf6:ed70:1fac:f2d2::/64) — v6 is reserved for future overlay AFs; underlay BGP today is v4-only
snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    lo0 {
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                address $LO0_V4;
            }
            family inet6 {
                address $LO0_V6;
            }
        }
    }
}

snips.name: tenant-gpu-server-link
snips.topic: Per-tenant GPU-server-facing 400G sub-port with dual-stack /31
highlights: Each GPU host attaches via 400G breakout sub-ports; each sub-port is bound to exactly ONE tenant VRF (clean per-tenant isolation, no sub-interfaces / VLANs needed)
Description carries the tenant tag (`VRF tenant-N to <host>`) so Apstra / operators can audit tenant-to-port mapping by `show interfaces`
Dual-stack: family inet with /31 + family inet6 (RAs handle v6 GUA via the router-advertisement snip)
MTU 9216 phy / 9170 inet — same as fabric P2P so VXLAN-encapsulated 9000B host frames pass without fragmentation across the entire path

pairWith.note: the VRF that owns this interface

snips.bodyHtml:
interfaces {
    $AC_INTF {
        description "VRF $TENANT_NAME to $GPU_HOST";
        mtu 9216;
        unit 0 {
            family inet {
                mtu 9170;
                address $LOCAL_V4;
            }
            family inet6;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: l2-learning-telemetry
snips.topic: L2-learning telemetry — stream remote MAC entries via gRPC
highlights: Enables export of remotely-learned MAC entries (i.e. EVPN-imported MACs from other leafs) via the streaming-telemetry channel
Without this, only locally-learned MACs are visible in the gRPC MAC-table feed, which makes EVPN MAC-mobility debugging blind
Leaf-only — spines don't terminate EVPN MAC entries in this design

pairWith.raw: evo/bootstrap/system-grpc-netconf.conf  (the gRPC channel that carries this telemetry)
pairWith.note: the gRPC channel that carries this telemetry

snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    l2-learning {
        telemetry {
            enable-remote-entries;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: lldp-interface-all
snips.topic: LLDP enabled on all interfaces with descriptive port-id reporting
highlights: `port-id-subtype interface-name` makes the advertised LLDP port-id match the actual Junos interface name (e.g. `et-0/0/0:0`) instead of an opaque ifIndex — much friendlier for Apstra / NMS topology discovery
`port-description-type interface-description` propagates the local interface description over LLDP, so neighbor `show lldp neighbors` surfaces things like "facing_spine1:et-0/0/0:0"
`neighbour-port-info-display port-id` (note British spelling) makes `show lldp neighbors` show the remote port-id instead of the remote description — useful when remote descriptions are stale
`interface all` covers every physical port; no need to enumerate
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    lldp {
        port-id-subtype interface-name;
        port-description-type interface-description;
        neighbour-port-info-display port-id;
        interface all;
    }
}

snips.name: rstp-disable
snips.topic: RSTP disabled on spine — pure L3 device, no L2 loop protection needed
highlights: Spines in this AI/ML CLOS are pure L3 (no IRB, no bridge-domain, no VLAN tagging) — RSTP would just emit BPDUs no one needs
Explicitly disabling (vs leaving default) silences the BPDU TX on every port and avoids wasted CPU on STP timers
Leafs do NOT include this stanza — they have IRBs and the default RSTP behavior is benign there since IRB ports aren't trunked
snips.bodyHtml:
protocols {
    rstp {
        disable;
    }
}

snips.name: allpodnetworks-direct-redistribution
snips.topic: AllPodNetworks — direct-route redistribution into BGP with community tagging
highlights: Redistributes locally-attached IPv4 + IPv6 direct routes into BGP and stamps them with the appropriate "default direct" community so downstream pods can identify their origin
Implicit-deny term at the bottom (term -100 reject) — prevents accidental leakage of any other route family
The companion `BGP-AOS-Policy` umbrella policy chains this one first, then accepts everything else from `protocol bgp` (spine) or only EVPN-derived /32-/32 + /128-/128 host routes (leaf)
Leafs use the same skeleton but extend it with per-tenant variants (see evo/policy/tenant-community-export.conf)
snips.bodyHtml:
/* --- SPINE variant (simpler — accepts all bgp-learned routes downstream) --- */
policy-options {
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks {
        term AllPodNetworks-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-20 {
            from protocol bgp;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

/* --- LEAF variant (only re-advertises host routes derived from EVPN) --- */
policy-options {
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks {
        term AllPodNetworks-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks;
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-50 {
            from {
                protocol evpn;
                route-filter 0.0.0.0/0 prefix-length-range /32-/32;
            }
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-60 {
            from {
                protocol evpn;
                route-filter 0::0/0 prefix-length-range /128-/128;
            }
            then accept;
        }
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: clos-loop-prevention
snips.topic: eBGP CLOS loop-prevention — spine tags outbound, leaf rejects tagged inbound
highlights: Standard 3-stage CLOS loop-prevention without relying on AS-path filtering
Spine stamps FROM_SPINE_*_TIER community on all routes sent to leafs
Leaf rejects any route carrying that stamp when exporting back to spines
Two parallel planes: one for fabric (inet unicast) and one for EVPN overlay

There's also a separate EVPN_EXPORT policy (leaf-only) that's a simple "accept all" stub — chained after LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT in the leaf's EVPN export so future filtering can be added without restructuring
Deploy the SPINE variant on spines, the LEAF variant on leafs — both sides reference the same community values
snips.bodyHtml:
/* --- SPINE variant (deploy on spine-role devices) --- */
policy-options {
    policy-statement SPINE_TO_LEAF_FABRIC_OUT {
        term SPINE_TO_LEAF_FABRIC_OUT-10 {
            then {
                community add FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER;
                accept;
            }
        }
    }
    policy-statement SPINE_TO_LEAF_EVPN_OUT {
        term SPINE_TO_LEAF_EVPN_OUT-10 {
            then {
                community add FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER;
                accept;
            }
        }
    }
}

/* --- LEAF variant (deploy on leaf-role devices) --- */
policy-options {
    policy-statement LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT {
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT-10 {
            from {
                protocol bgp;
                community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER;
            }
            then reject;
        }
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_FABRIC_OUT-20 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
    policy-statement LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT {
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT-10 {
            from {
                protocol bgp;
                community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER;
            }
            then reject;
        }
        term LEAF_TO_SPINE_EVPN_OUT-20 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
    policy-statement EVPN_EXPORT {
        term EVPN_EXPORT-4095 {
            then accept;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: community-definitions
snips.topic: BGP community definitions — tier markers, default redistribution, per-tenant
highlights: FROM_SPINE_*_TIER (0:14, 0:15) = CLOS loop-prevention markers stamped by spines and rejected by leafs (see clos-loop-prevention snip)
DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4/V6 = identifies locally-redistributed direct routes; the high-order tag (1:* on spines, 5:* on leafs) encodes the tier, the low-order tag is the family selector + a fabric ID
TENANT-N_COMMUNITY_V4/V6 = per-tenant VRF marker carried on EVPN type-5 routes; pairs with the per-tenant export policies
NON-TENANT_COMMUNITY_V4/V6 = shared / infrastructure routes that need cross-tenant visibility
Tier prefix differs by role: spines use `1:20007` / `1:20008`, leafs use `5:20007` / `5:20008` for the same DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4/V6
All other communities are identical between leaf and spine — keep only the tier-specific DEFAULT_DIRECT lines per role
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    /* Tier markers — identical on every device in the fabric */
    community FROM_SPINE_EVPN_TIER members 0:14;
    community FROM_SPINE_FABRIC_TIER members 0:15;

    /* Direct-route redistribution — high-order tag differs per role */
    community DEFAULT_DIRECT_V4 members [ ${LOCAL_TIER_TAG}:20007 21001:26000 ];
    community DEFAULT_DIRECT_V6 members [ ${LOCAL_TIER_TAG}:20008 21001:26000 ];

    /* Per-tenant + non-tenant markers — leaf-only in this JVD */
    community NON-TENANT_COMMUNITY_V4 members [ 5:20007 21010:26000 ];
    community NON-TENANT_COMMUNITY_V6 members [ 5:20008 21010:26000 ];
    community TENANT-1_COMMUNITY_V4 members [ 5:20007 21002:26000 ];
    community TENANT-1_COMMUNITY_V6 members [ 5:20008 21002:26000 ];
    community TENANT-2_COMMUNITY_V4 members [ 5:20007 21003:26000 ];
    community TENANT-2_COMMUNITY_V6 members [ 5:20008 21003:26000 ];
    community TENANT-3_COMMUNITY_V4 members [ 5:20007 21004:26000 ];
    community TENANT-3_COMMUNITY_V6 members [ 5:20008 21004:26000 ];
    community TENANT-4_COMMUNITY_V4 members [ 5:20007 21005:26000 ];
    community TENANT-4_COMMUNITY_V6 members [ 5:20008 21005:26000 ];
}

snips.name: pfe-load-balance
snips.topic: PFE per-packet load-balance policy (referenced by forwarding-table export)
highlights: Tells the FIB to install per-packet load-balance next-hops — the actual hashing/DLB behavior is governed by forwarding-options
Without this policy, ECMP routes default to per-prefix load-balance (only one of the available next-hops is used per prefix)
Required by the DLB flowlet engine to even see all the parallel paths it can shift flows between
snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement PFE-LB {
        then {
            load-balance per-packet;
        }
    }
}

snips.name: tenant-community-export
snips.topic: Per-tenant export policies — AllPodNetworks-tenant-N + BGP-AOS-Policy-tenant-N
highlights: One pair of policies per tenant VRF — they're referenced by the VRF's `evpn ip-prefix-routes export` statement (NOT by the global BGP underlay group)
Each tenant gets a unique community pair (V4/V6) — listed below as `non-tenant`, `tenant-1`, `tenant-2`, `tenant-3`, `tenant-4`. Add new tenants by cloning the block and incrementing the index.
The structure is identical across tenants — only the tenant tag in the policy name + the community name change. A future jinja template should fold these into a single per-tenant generator.
The `non-tenant` variant exists for shared / infrastructure routes that need to be visible across tenants (e.g. a shared storage VRF leaked into tenant-N via auto-export)

pairWith.note: consumer of these per-tenant export policies

snips.bodyHtml:
policy-options {
    policy-statement AllPodNetworks-$TENANT_TAG {
        term AllPodNetworks-${TENANT_TAG}-10 {
            from {
                family inet;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $TENANT_COMMUNITY_V4;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-${TENANT_TAG}-20 {
            from {
                family inet6;
                protocol direct;
            }
            then {
                community add $TENANT_COMMUNITY_V6;
                accept;
            }
        }
        term AllPodNetworks-${TENANT_TAG}-100 {
            then reject;
        }
    }
    policy-statement BGP-AOS-Policy-$TENANT_TAG {
        term BGP-AOS-Policy-${TENANT_TAG}-10 {
            from policy AllPodNetworks-$TENANT_TAG;
            then accept;
